The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine?utm_source=chatgpt.com</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>

These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>

# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>

Go to [[:en:Wikipedia:Requests for undeletion]] to request undeletion of original page [[:en:draft:Tokusa-no-Kandakara]][[en:draft:Tokusa-no-Kandakara]] and import the revisions to here for attribution. View on everybodywiki here [[:every:Tokusa-no-Kandakara|Tokusa-no-Kandakara]]. [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref>
[[ja:十種神宝]]
In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 21:01, 16 February 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|1=This page was originally created by Immanuelle as a wikipedia draft. It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

revision.text:
{{複数の問題|出典の明記=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:03 (UTC)|独自研究=2017年8月28日 (月) 08:03 (UTC)}} '''十種神宝'''（とくさのかんだから）は、『[[先代旧事本紀]]』「天孫本紀」（巻3）に'''天璽瑞宝十種'''（あまつしるし-みずたから-とくさ）として登場する10種類の[[宝|宝物]]。記述によると[[ニギハヤヒ|饒速日命]]（にぎはやひのみこと）が天降りする際に、天神御祖（あまつかみみおや）から授けられたとする。
== 概要 == 分類すれば、[[鏡]]2種、[[剣]]1種、[[勾玉|玉]]4種、比礼（女性が首に結ばずに掛け、左右から同じ長さで前に垂らす[[スカーフ]]様のもの）3種となる。これを[[三種の神器]]に対応させて、鏡は[[八咫鏡]]、剣と比礼は[[天叢雲剣|草薙剣]]、玉は[[八尺瓊勾玉]]であるとする説もある。
十種神宝の内容は以下の通りである。 *沖津鏡（おきつかがみ） *辺津鏡（へつかがみ） *八握剣（やつかのつるぎ） *生玉（いくたま） *死返玉（まかるかへしのたま） *足玉（たるたま） *道返玉（ちかへしのたま） *蛇比礼（おろちのひれ）…[[大国主の神話]]に出てくる比礼との関係が注目される。 *蜂比礼（はちのひれ）…大国主の神話に出てくる比礼との関係が注目される。 *品物之比礼（くさぐさのもののひれ）
== あまつしるし == 十種神宝は『[[記紀]]』には出てこないが、[[天津神]]の子であることを示す「あまつしるし」は、『記紀』ともに[[神武天皇]]の段に登場し、『[[古語拾遺]]』などにも記載がある。[[漢字]]は[[古事記]]は「天津瑞」、[[日本書紀]]は「天表」、[[古語拾遺]]は「天璽」とそれぞれ当てている。各記述の相違は以下である。
*古事記 - 単に天津瑞とだけしている。 *日本書紀 -「[[天羽々矢]]（あまのははや）」と「[[うつぼ#靱、靫|歩靱（かちゆき）]]」。武具らしき物を天表としている。 *古語拾遺 - 現在も皇位の表徴として有名な、[[三種の神器]]を天璽としている。
== 布瑠の言 == 布瑠の言（ふるのこと）とは、「ひふみ祓詞」・「ひふみ神言」ともいい、死者蘇生の言霊といわれる。
== 十種神宝の行方 == [[石上神宮]]の祭神である布留御魂神は十種神宝のことであるとする説もある。石上神宮に伝わる鎮魂法では「ひふみの祓詞」や十種神宝の名前を唱える。
本物か不明であるが、[[大阪市]][[平野区]]喜連6丁目にある[[式内楯原神社]]内の神寶十種之宮に、偶然、町の古道具屋で発見されたという十種神宝が祀られている。石上神宮側から返還要請があったにもかかわらず、返していないという。
[[江戸時代]]、山崎闇斎は、垂加神道においては神秘的な意義の有るものとして、さまざまな口伝的著述を残した。
[[籠神社]]には、息津鏡・辺津鏡という2面の鏡が伝世している。十種神宝の沖津鏡・辺津鏡との関係は不明で、籠神社も特に見解は出していない。
[[秋田県]][[大仙市]]の唐松神社には[[古史古伝]]のひとつである『物部文書』とともに奥津鏡、辺津鏡、十握の剣、生玉、足玉とされる物が所蔵されているという。
京都市伏見区には伏見神寳神社がある。
== 関連項目 == [[File:Grand Cordon of the Order of the Sacred Treasure 001.jpg|thumb|勲一等瑞宝章（瑞宝大綬章）の正章]] ; 十種神宝 * [[神器]] * [[瑞宝章]] - 意匠は宇摩志麻治命が神武天皇に奉った神宝にちなむとされる。 ; 布瑠の言 * [[言霊]] ; 関連作品 * [[足洗邸の住人たち。]] * [[永遠かもしれない]] * [[双界儀]] * [[かくりよものがたり]] * [[ザ・キング・オブ・ファイターズ]] - 『KOF京』および『KOF EX』作中において、草薙、八神（八尺瓊）、神楽（八咫）の「三種の神器」一族を守護する宿命を持つ者たち。作中での読みは『KOF京』では「じっしゅのしんぽう」、『KOF EX』では「とくさのかむだから」。 * [[大神 (ゲーム)]] - 作中の武器のそれぞれの名前は、実際の三種の神器や十種神宝から来ている。 * [[呪術廻戦]] - 伏黒恵の術式「十種影法術」の元ネタ。
== 参考文献 == *{{Cite book |和書 |author=進藤孝一 |title=秋田「物部文書」伝承 |publisher=無明舎出版 |year=1984 |date=1984-01 |doi=10.11501/12237704 |id={{国立国会図書館書誌ID|000001689378}} |ref=harv }}
{{神道 横}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:とくさのかんたから}} [[Category:日本神話]] [[Category:神話・伝説の道具]] [[Category:日本の名数10|かんたから]] {{Shinto-stub}}

Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

It was then automatically transferred to everybodywiki and recovered from there. In the future it will be good to import the revisions. Check page history in case this page was moved, since the everybodywiki page may have been under a different title[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 20:10, 11 February 2025 (UTC)}}
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:十種神宝]][[zh:十種神寶]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:十種神宝]][[zh:十種神寶]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:十種神宝]][[zh:十種神寶]]
The '''Tokusa-no-Kandakara''' (十種神宝), also known as the Ten Sacred Treasures, are a set of ten divine artifacts in Japanese mythology. These treasures were bestowed upon Nigihayahi no Mikoto, an ancestral deity of the Mononobe clan, by the heavenly deities (Amatsu Kami) of Takamagahara.<ref>https://shintoinari.org/qa/what-is-tokusa-no-kamdakara/</ref>
The ten treasures are categorized into four classes: two mirrors, one sword, four magatama (comma-shaped beads), and three scarves (hire). Each item holds significant spiritual and symbolic importance:
# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
== References == <references />{{Isonokami Faith}}

# '''Okitsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Deep) # '''Hetsu-kagami''' (Mirror of the Shore) # '''Yatsuka-no-Tsurugi''' (Eight Hands Long Sword) # '''Iku-tama''' (Jewel of Life) # '''Makaru-kaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Resuscitation) # '''Taru-tama''' (Jewel of Plenty) # '''Chikaeshi-no-tama''' (Jewel of Turning Back on the Road) # '''Orochi-no-hire''' (Snake Scarf) # '''Hachi-no-hire''' (Bee Scarf) # '''Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire''' (Scarf to Ward Off Various Things)
These treasures were believed to possess magical properties, including the power to revive the dead when their names were chanted while waving them.<ref>https://www.japanesewiki.com/literature/Tokusanokandakara.html</ref> In contemporary culture, the concept of the Ten Sacred Treasures has influenced various media. For instance, in the manga and anime series "Jujutsu Kaisen," the character Megumi Fushiguro utilizes the '''Ten Shadows Technique''' (十種影法術, Tokusa no Kage Bōjutsu), which allows him to summon ten different shikigami (spiritual creatures).<ref>https://jujutsu-kaisen.fandom.com/wiki/Ten_Shadows_Technique</ref>
The Tokusa-no-Kandakara are also associated with the Isonokami Shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan, where they are believed to have been housed.<ref>https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Isonokami_Shrine</ref>
These artifacts continue to be a subject of interest in both historical studies and popular culture, symbolizing Japan's rich mythological heritage.
